title: @nanyubusnis9397
text:
37:15 And I'm convinced you would have said the opposite if you started out constructing things like this, and then discovered the beauty of fitting everything into nice squares/rectangles.
See, what this really is, is a new challenge to rekindle your love for this game, or more precisely, the base-building-and-automation part of the game.
The reason this feels so satisfying is because you're just solving a different logistics puzzle. One that is much more "I build as I go" than one that is completely pre-planned. Pre-planning ofcourse has the advantage of making everything fit nicely, and the end view being nicely organized and looking good. The advantage of this jumbled mess of machinery is that you're essentially expanding the puzzle yourself, and it's probably because of how much experience you have with the game and the recipes that you're able to have this much fun. Imagine the nightmare this would be for a newbie. Finally managing to fit things into your unplanned spaghetti only to find out you need space for another belt, or no room for those electric wires. Or, don't imagine, remember. xP
All of this to say: Glad you're having fun. It's been fun watching another one of your videos. Or should I say: It has been satisfying.

title: @Failuigi41
text:
You inspired me to give factorio another go. I decided, in my infinite wisdom, to make modular blocks to generate 90spm (blue assemblers, no modules) of every science from raw ores.

I made the mistake of taking the rate calculator as word-of-god, and quickly discovered that my final design for yellow science was suffering an inexplicable lack of copper plates for a reason i haven't yet deciphered.

The solution? Set up circuit conditions and spaghetti that reroutes any and all excess copper, from EVERY other part of the build that eats copper,  back through the entire build to be sprinkled in riiight before the haunted green circuit block. 

I like to think you would be proud of the mess i have made. I kept hearing your voice in my head, whispering that there must be SOME way to route these 20 undergrounds directly through an entire furnace block. And you were right every time. Truly, nothing can stop a truly determined engineer from getting junk from point A to point B

title: @RoyalFusilier
text: It's genuinely beautiful, even factories can be works of art and this one is right up there. It helps that this sort of spaghetti style, perhaps a bit less extreme, is also how I learned the game originally in the time before legend from that OG Deathworld series I've mentioned before. He played on the patch Oil was introduced, and Warlord Mal just didn't have the time, space, nor resources for scaling industry up. So I ended up copying the style even in my normal settings runs. I also appreciated making low-pollution bases that drew almost no Biter attacks, just for my own peace of mind. The default modern meta of gigantic expansion and endless scalable efficiency is something I've learned as like a quaint new playstyle in recent years.

Challenge inspired by the opposite of my solution to this problem
No leeching or expanding
Ores, water, and crude oil only, and if you need an item you need to make a whole base/wing specifically for that one item. Each item made needs its own individual, independent processing for its ingredients, so one massive smelting wing is out of the question. Explosives? Need its own oil refinery and chemical plants, can't just take some from the other plastic plant. Sorta...Hypermodular.

title: @Wiz_theGamer
text: 30:32 Wouldn't a single arithmetic combinator be enough? Think about it. It is also subject to the one tick delay. Therefore if you feed a red wire from the train stop directly into the alarm and a green wire into an arithmetic combinator, invert the value and then send it through a red wire to the alarm. The result will also be a single tick of T. Which then when the train leaves is for a single tick negativ, which could also be used with the condition T != 0 to make a sound when a train leaves, but with your condition it would result in the same thing happening and save on resources as well

title: @RedstoneDefender
text:
I think this is great, but I'm not so sure it's true spaghetti. It's too refined, because you know all the ratios and the requirements of things. That you can set things up with confidence that you will fulfill the needs given to you. That when you need to, you replace previous sections with a better version. 
Perhaps this style could be called "Coral" rather than spaghetti, building out in a organic-ish fashion, but rather than rebuilding section after section, getting lost and confused, you instead add new growth to fill a new need.

text:
Great video and mod!

Now I'm really curious: If you could choose the previous or next Fibonacci number for parallel buildings, how would a fabric grow and look like? Then the rule might work for belts, too.

If 2 buildings are parallel, the next parallel buildings could be 3 of the type. And next to the 3 buildings could be 2 or 5 parallel buildings. And to the 5 buildings could be 3 or 8 parallel buildings, and so on.
Fibonnaci numbers are a common phenomenon in many things, such as art oder nature (and maybe more).

I guess a fabric could look more artistical and or "natural grown". But I could be be completely wrong about that :D

title: @aukemebel4263
text:
Currently playing Krastorio 2/Space Exploration with randomised properties and recipe amounts.
It wasn't too bad until I got to the space platform and space science, because the amounts of cooling fluids you need for science production was usually 10 in/10 out.
But now the recipe for 25 to -10 , -10 to -100, and -100 to -273 give always a bit too much, so most of my recipe are overflowing me on Thermofluid.
This has got me to make trains specifically to travel it to the planet to flarestack/air filter it out of existence.
I can tell you that this combination of mods has turned this already hard mod pack of 600 hours into a close to 1000 hour mess.
Keeps me busy though.
Analysis paralysis is real though.

title: @KerbalRocketry
text:
I've never understood bidirectional rail hate, provided you completely understand safe path-signalling you're fine :3 <- (girl with over a thousand hours spent playing OTTD before she even touched factorio, remembers the pre-pathsignal days, and who's game is featured on the OTTD steam page)

But unironically i think they're really good and more people should use them, it massively reduces resources for long distances and you Do Not Need the capacity of double track. a few passing loops if plenty to make a single track have wayy more throughput than most players need. Upgrading the core to double is sensible yes, but honestly if you can't build bidirectional tracks you can't build the sort of junction that would be needed to handle the traffic of actually heavily used double track.

text: I know you're joking, but that pattern of "the idiot and the master act / think similarly, yet the moderately skilled person doesn't" holds surprisingly often. So, whenever you see idiots claiming X and you're moderately informed and disagree, it's possible that actually the joke is on you because you're overlooking that the really smart people are on the idiot's side. (Although it doesn't help that MSM tends to censor really smart people when they disagree with the narrative.)

title: @NaiyaTheTiger
text: ​ @lightworker2956 There's usually an important level of knowledge/ skill difference that lets the master get away with it, without running into the same problems. In this case, Dosh can get away with the spaghetti because he's played enough to memorize the ratios and know what to expect. Newer players are still figuring it out, and so are constantly having to add more production, that they may not have left enough space for.

title: @Menolith
text: Yeah, the main benefit of a main bus is that you don't need much foreknowledge about the tech tree, so you can just peel belts off and add them to the bus as you go. The more you play, the better understanding you get about the ratios and levels of demand you'll have in the future, so there's less need for the kind of organizational simplicity a main bus gives you.

>I don't have a mechanical mind and the creation of systems lit up no firework in my brain. Instead i spent 15minutes tending to the exact placement of the trees in relation to my buildings then a hour paving sections using all three layers to be realistic or good looking yet the factory demanded like a spoiled child tearing me away from the fun i was trying to have and the biters.
>I never had a moment to myself to do anything enjoyable like placing single walls and the side of a road.
>Straitening my belts and my pipes or twisting them when appropriate, making sure no sprites overlap, drawing nicer coastlines, uncrooking my rails and minding the spacing of them, the thickness of my walls, the balance of industrial and natural elements.
>I was in the business of creating a world, a map, a level i would want to walk through. An efficient factory is useless to me, i hate the factory, it takes everything and gives nothing back. I don't know why i took this game so personally but i felt abused by it.
>I'm French.

title: @Nechto_zloe
text:
Some of my mindustry bases, while on a lesser scale, fit in this design. In that game you are forced to build small because the bigger the base, the bigger the defence budjet(and locals are a bit scarier than biters), so you want 2 chokes max, and you are forced to build diagonally, because some building's shapes are awkward, and walls(and patches) are mostly wobbly. And you have a lot less map space in general. Also core feeding is a thing, which very much promotes tumor style cores. So the result is like:
-Tumor core, a million lines going in and out
-Some local unit production, strapped onto a perfect silicon octagon, like an exess limb on a whale
-Cursed metaglass furnace, feeding straight into 5 turrets, and a belt to 7 more turrets
-3 sushi belts on top one another
-A mine in the middle of a turret stack
-2km long line of oil processing
-45 accumulators, blocking the way for units
-Starter turrets no longer fed by ammo, with belts on top.
-A poodle of tier 1 air units you forgot you set up, hovering above the factory

Idea for a video: Space age but for the off planet resource chains, every step on the chain MUST take place on a different planet.

So for example you would have to mine scrap on fulgora, transport it to Vulcanus to recycle it, get the Holmium ore and sent it to Nauvis to make the solution, take the Holmium solution to Gleba to turn it into electrolyte, then you can take that to Aquilo and make the science pack, then take it back to Nauvis to put it in a lab.

title: @Leyrann
text:
That steel build for the purple science looks scarily like the demonic builds used by speedrunners.

By the way, an easier way of making this work, and one that cannot possibly fail, is to use a single (yellow) inserter to drop fuel onto the belt, and then feed any fuel that reaches the end back into the original fuel belt with priority. This way, the fuel will just keep looping and there can never be more fuel on the lane than the inserter's capacity. No circuit logic necessary! The only way in which this can kind of ""fail"" is if the steel output is backed up, but this by definition means that the furnaces will turn off, and once steel starts flowing again, the lane will empty and the furnaces can grab new fuel (in practice, if the iron ore/iron plates continue flowing, the steel smelting furnaces will run out of fuel when they have ~60 steel in them, meaning they have a significant buffer to unload before any new fuel is necessary, and if they have room on the belt to unload, then there is by definition room for fuel to reach them).

title: @ZealotOfSteal
text:
An absolutely gorgeous base, but it has a different charm to a vanilla spaghetti base.
What I mean is a base that starts ordered and with some empty space, but over time thoughts like "maybe I can fit a small build for X here" and "I need another belt of inputs here" fills up available space and creates a barely working mesh of machines. 
Snaking in another belt of iron plates halfway through a poorly thought out main bus is a fun experience.

Then you reach peak spaghetti, where modifying the whole mess in any way creates a whole chain of problems so long that it would be easier and faster to just abandon the whole thing and start from scratch. 
There's a very specific charm to bases like that, where you can see the initial design that was slowly overtaken by growths, modified to fit in something else and so on.

5:23 the crashed ship has the placeable-off-grid flag on it, meaning it doesn't have to be aligned to the grid. thus, you can end up in a situation where an inserter can be placed on a tile, because they have tiny hitboxes, but a larger building can't be because it takes up a larger part of the tile. the fluid box input doesn't affect anything here.

title: @AdrianHereToHelp
text:
I don't generally play gacha games, but I've been trying out Arknights: Endfield, which has some very space-limited factory areas you have to build in (and also doesn't have underground belts). I of course wanted to build a huge complex that automates literally every item that can be automated in the first area (at ⅓ uptime, since my math told me it wouldn't be possible to go higher without running out of space). The sheer compact spaghetti required to get it all to work and be powered wasn't that far off from this lmao
This was in part because I insisted on automating all the flora production I needed in a single smaller-area outpost, which is only 40x40 tiles (relevant buildings are 5x5 and I needed 38 of them, and there's a mandatory 9x9 "main building", plus any extra 3x3 output buildings I might need). I spent hours on that, and when I finally got it properly working I felt like a god

I feel like if you really want to capture the kind of spaghetti you get in newer bases, ratios kill that. You know exactly how much you need of something and therefore always build the right amount. title: @nanyubusnis9397
text:
37:15 And I'm convinced you would have said the opposite if you started out constructing things like this, and then discovered the beauty of fitting everything into nice squares/rectangles.
See, what this really is, is a new challenge to rekindle your love for this game, or more precisely, the base-building-and-automation part of the game.
The reason this feels so satisfying is because you're just solving a different logistics puzzle. One that is much more "I build as I go" than one that is completely pre-planned. Pre-planning ofcourse has the advantage of making everything fit nicely, and the end view being nicely organized and looking good. The advantage of this jumbled mess of machinery is that you're essentially expanding the puzzle yourself, and it's probably because of how much experience you have with the game and the recipes that you're able to have this much fun. Imagine the nightmare this would be for a newbie. Finally managing to fit things into your unplanned spaghetti only to find out you need space for another belt, or no room for those electric wires. Or, don't imagine, remember. xP
All of this to say: Glad you're having fun. It's been fun watching another one of your videos. Or should I say: It has been satisfying.

I love this, it tickles my brain in the right way.
This is probably the main reason why Mindustry is my favorite factory builder, the game actually encourages designs like this over scalability and flexibility considering that you are forced to small spaces with irregular shapes and belt throughput isn't enough to justify a main bus.
Even if you are just copy pasting blueprints, the optimal designs there are incomprehensible messes that take raw ores as input (often with drills built into the design) and outputs the final product, and it all is somehow a perfect rectangle with no holes.
Kind of tangent but whatever

title: @rogo7330
text:
Laser turrets are ass, and you need "reload" them anyway, because they will get damaged and you will need to supply defences with spare parts, bots and repair packs (optionally). Flame turres are cool, but they are just scam for early game. Not as much as regular ammunition turrets, but close because they demand SO MUCH material to build something it would be actually cheaper to build a tank and kill everything in the vicinity yourself.

The answer - just build mines. Even early game, one or two assemblers and fields planted by hand will hold biters for very long time, and it's very cheap (not sure if cheaper than ammo-less laser turrets, but it's really low). And they are auto-reloadable by bots too. And you don't need to build walls, like at all. You can even just build tanks with solar panels and personal robopots in them, drive them via map view to the defence position and forget about them until biters start explode tanks instead of mines.

title: @gammaplayz4434
text:
New challenge: Make a pizza base. Instead of moving resources to the machines, move the machines to the resources. Each factory has to be visible 1 fully zoomed in screen when standing on an ore-tile. Play on many, small and poor patches. You are allowed to import raw resources to other patches (Belt copper to an iron patch for Red Science), but no duplicates per factory (2 copper patches for one factory, not okay. 1 iron, 1 stone, 1 uranium, 1 oil is okay). No trains or wide-area bot networks for item logistics, but both trains and bots are allowed for malls and personal use. Multiple power grids isn't a requirement, but HIGHLY encouraged (Remember, more patches/factory is okay). Bonus completion if the bases are square-ish, Bonus bonus completion of they are round.

Crust = walls
Toppings = Mini-factories
Sauce = All the pain and suffering

title: @Azrage
text: 50:00 While this may not fit the spaghetti spirit, one trick I've used a few times is that you can get away with only one tile blocked off for fluid inputs on assemblers by having the main pipe further out and using underground pipes to get the fluid into the assembler. Obviously it's much more expensive, but it can be helpful. You can also use normal pipes with a one block gap and long inserters to get a belt and pipe right next to the machines, which avoids excessive undergrounds.

title: @whtiequillBj
text:
I'm going to preface this comment by saying; this is one of my favorite videos you have made!

Have you considered doing a spaghetti with randomizer?
For this challenge you are allowed to look over all the recipes and make sure everything is actually doable in a reasonable run rather than just going in completely blind.

What I enjoy is watching the strategy and how you make things work together. Rather than simply you bashing your head against a wall trying to figure out why it takes 10,000 ammo to make 20,000 guns.

So if you have a pre-planned strategy based on materials and development costs from what the randomizer gives you and you've planned, how the randomizer is going to work. After looking at the bill of materials, I think that would also be very interesting to see how you plan around what you already now know rather than a completely blind run.

So the idea is you get a random bill of materials which you choose from maybe five different seeds. You spend a week studying the bill of materials you chose and understanding how the bill of materials works and then you do a run.

title: @gayforbrae5693
text:
Honestly spaghetti isn’t something you set out to make 
You can’t code the game to prevent you from not making spaghetti because the spaghetti is a result of your brain not the game
Spaghetti isn’t a style of building it’s a style of approaching the game and thinking 
Ive made some absolute messes especially with pyanodons 
It’s always looking at something and going yea I can use this space for that 
And then realising that the space is too small for that thing but not giving up on it and just hoping it’ll sort itself out 
Or it’s the absolute refusal to use busses because they’re dull and boring so you instead shove a belt through a completely random area 
Then of course you need that item for something else so you just tack it on to that belt in another random area to produce yet another belt of shit that needs to be routed through the middle of everything 
It’s just one of those things that you can’t approach creating with coding or anything like that 
You have to mentally change and just change the very Basis of how you play the game
